questions.question: What is a Turing machine?
options: A theoretical model of computation using an infinite tape and read/write head
The first electronic computer, built in 1943
A quantum computer that processes probabilistic states
A neural network architecture developed at MIT
questions.funFact: Alan Turing described this abstract machine in 1936 — before electronic computers existed — as a thought experiment to define the limits of what can be computed.

questions.question: Norbert Wiener founded the field of cybernetics in 1948. What did he study?
options: The mathematics of quantum entanglement
Early programming languages for analog computers
Control and communication systems in animals and machines
The sociology of technological unemployment
questions.funFact: Wiener warned in 1950 that automation would cause massive unemployment — over 70 years before today's AI debates. He was largely ignored.

questions.question: In a transformer model — the architecture behind modern LLMs — what is the mechanism that allows the model to weigh the relevance of different words to each other?
options: Backpropagation
Gradient descent
Self-attention
Recurrent state
questions.funFact: The 2017 paper 'Attention Is All You Need' introduced transformers and is now one of the most cited papers in the history of computer science.

questions.question: Where did the very first Nerd Nite take place?
options: New York City
San Francisco
Boston
London
questions.funFact: The first Nerd Nite was at The Midway bar in Boston in 2003, where scientist Chris Balakrishnan started sharing his field research stories with bar patrons.

questions.question: According to Nerd Nite lore, why did the bartenders at The Midway suggest Chris start the event?
options: They wanted to charge a cover on slow weeknights
A local university offered to sponsor the bar for science outreach
They were tired of overhearing him retell the same stories to different friends every night
Chris lost a bet and had to give a public lecture
questions.funFact: Chris was studying indigo birds in Cameroon — a parasitic species — and would return each fall and spend weeks at The Midway retelling the same expedition stories.

questions.question: How many cities worldwide currently host Nerd Nite events?
options: Around 25
About 50
Over 100
Nearly 250
questions.funFact: Nerd Nite has spread to over 100 cities on six continents, making it one of the most successful grassroots science communication formats ever created.

talks.title: Star Trek Trivia — USS Cordilleran Edition
talks.speaker: USS Cordilleran

questions.question: What is the Prime Directive in Star Trek?
options: A Starfleet order to explore new worlds and expand Federation territory
A principle of non-interference with the natural development of alien civilizations
The command hierarchy aboard a Federation starship
A treaty between the Federation and the Klingon Empire
questions.funFact: The Prime Directive has been violated by Captain Kirk alone so many times that Starfleet Command reportedly has a special file just for him.

questions.question: What does the stardate system in Star Trek represent?
options: A precise timekeeping system based on stellar positions relative to Earth
The number of days since the founding of the United Federation of Planets
A Vulcan calendar adopted by the Federation in the 22nd century
A deliberately vague fictional timekeeping system — intentionally undefined
questions.funFact: Gene Roddenberry made stardates intentionally meaningless so the show wouldn't be tied to a fixed timeline. Different Trek series use completely incompatible numbering systems.

questions.question: In a 1993 TNG episode, warp travel was established as environmentally damaging. What did it damage?
options: The antimatter containment fields of nearby vessels
The subspace fabric of space itself
The gravitational stability of planetary systems
The dilithium deposits of inhabited worlds
questions.funFact: The episode 'Force of Nature' set a warp speed limit of 9 to protect subspace — a plot point the writers quietly dropped and almost never mentioned again.

originStory.text:
Welcome to Nerdometer.

Tonight is a little different. There's no main stage talk — just a planning session, a community conversation, and this: a live demo of the trivia game I built for Nerd Nite Fort Collins.

You're the beta testers. We want to know what works, what doesn't, and what we should do more of.

Let's play.

Nerd Nite Fort Collins runs on a small crew.

Justin Fritz — that's me — Director and Executive Producer.
Senne Van Loon — Producer and Talent Management.
Jamie Fritz — Brand Management and Treasurer.
Hannah Bauer — Host and Brand Ambassador.

We put this on because we believe Fort Collins deserves a place where curious people can come together, learn something weird, and talk to strangers about it over a beer.

Wolverine Farm is that place.

One thing we need to talk about tonight:

Wolverine Farm's Event Hall — where we run our show — is not ADA accessible.

That's not acceptable to us. We are launching a fundraising effort to fix it. We're calling it the Wolverine Farm Lift Fund.

If you have ideas, connections, grant writing experience, or just want to help — find us after the game. We are going to think about it. Then we are going to do it.

Trivia done. But you're not done yet.

One more round — the bonus round. It's fast, it's different, and it can move you up the leaderboard.

Your trivia score is locked in. Now let's see what else you've got.
